title: Database Replication System
instructions: Implement master-slave and master-master replication for the LevelDB socket server. Simpler than MySQL - use binary log (binlog) stored in LevelDB, replication protocol over TCP sockets, and automatic recovery on reconnection.

1783140379.6749635.instruction:
Add command-line arguments to server.py:
- --server-id ID (unique identifier for this server, e.g., 1, 2, 3)
- --role (master or slave, default master)
- --master-host HOST:PORT (for slaves, where to connect)
- --replication-port PORT (separate port for replication connections)

Store these in the SocketServer class. When role=slave, server starts in slave mode and will connect to master.

1783140379.7135623.instruction:
Create new file binlog.py that implements binary logging:

1. Binlog class that writes to LevelDB (separate db: data/_binlog)
2. Each write operation logs an entry with:
   - position (auto-incrementing integer)
   - timestamp
   - server_id (who made the change)
   - database name
   - operation type (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE_DB, DROP_DB, CREATE_TABLE, DROP_TABLE)
   - table name (if applicable)
   - data (the actual row data or query params)

3. Methods:
   - write_entry(op_type, db_name, table_name, data) -> returns position
   - get_entries(from_position, limit=100) -> list of entries
   - get_latest_position() -> current position

Modify database.py INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE_DB, DROP_DB, CREATE_TABLE, DROP_TABLE to call binlog.write_entry() after successful operation.

1783140379.713798.instruction:
1. Extend auth.py to support REPLICATION SLAVE privilege
2. Add new command to create replication users:
   CREATE REPLICATION USER 'repl'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY 'secret';
3. This creates a user with only REPLICATION SLAVE privilege (cannot query data)
4. Add SHOW MASTER STATUS command that returns current binlog position

Update CommandRegistry with:
- CREATE_REPL_USER command
- SHOW_MASTER_STATUS command

1783140379.7139697.instruction:
Create new file replication.py with replication protocol:

1. ReplicationClient class (runs on slave):
   - connect_to_master(host, port, username, password)
   - send: REPL AUTH user pass
   - send: REPL STREAM FROM position
   - receive binlog entries continuously
   - apply each entry to local database

2. ReplicationHandler class (runs on master):
   - Handles incoming replication connections
   - Authenticates replication user
   - Streams binlog entries from requested position
   - Sends: REPL ENTRY position timestamp server_id db table op data
   - Heartbeat every 30 seconds

3. When slave receives entry:
   - Parse the operation
   - Execute on local database (bypass binlog writing to avoid loops)
   - Track last_applied_position

Modify server.py to start ReplicationClient when role=slave.

1783140379.7141051.instruction:
Add SQL-like commands for replication control:

1. START SLAVE - Begin replication (connect to master, start streaming)
2. STOP SLAVE - Pause replication (keep connection, stop applying)
3. RESET SLAVE - Clear replication state, start from beginning
4. SHOW SLAVE STATUS - Show:
   - Master host:port
   - Connection status (connected/disconnected)
   - Last received position
   - Last applied position
   - Seconds behind master

Update CommandRegistry with these new commands and implement in commands.py.

1783140379.7142265.instruction:
1. Slave stores last applied position in LevelDB (data/_slave_state)
2. On startup, slave reads last position and resumes from there
3. If connection to master drops:
   - Retry connection every 5 seconds
   - On reconnect, request entries from last_applied_position + 1
4. If master was down and comes back:
   - Slave automatically reconnects
   - Catches up on missed entries
5. Handle network errors gracefully with exponential backoff

Add to ReplicationClient:
- save_position(position)
- load_position() -> returns last saved position
- reconnect_with_backoff()

1783140379.7143438.instruction:
Extend replication to support master-master:

1. Both servers have --role=master and --peer-host HOST:PORT
2. Each server writes to its own binlog with its server-id
3. When replicating, skip entries with own server-id (avoid loops)
4. Conflict resolution:
   - Last-write-wins based on timestamp
   - Or: reject conflicting writes from peer if local is newer
5. Each server acts as both master (accepts writes) and slave (receives from peer)

Modify ReplicationClient to:
- Skip entries where entry['server_id'] == self.server_id
- Handle conflicts based on timestamp

1783140379.7144656.instruction:
Add visibility into replication:

1. SHOW MASTER STATUS shows:
   - Current binlog file (always 1 in our case)
   - Current position
   - Number of connected slaves

2. SHOW SLAVE STATUS shows:
   - Master host:port
   - Slave IO state (connecting/connected/error)
   - Slave SQL state (running/stopped)
   - Last error message
   - Seconds behind master (lag)

3. Log replication events to console:
   - "Slave connected from 192.168.1.100"
   - "Replicating entry 1050: INSERT INTO users..."
   - "Slave lag: 2 seconds"

1783140379.7145884.instruction:
Create test script tests/test_replication.py:

1. Start master server on port 9999
2. Start slave server on port 9998, pointing to master
3. Connect to master, insert data
4. Verify data appears on slave
5. Stop slave, insert more data on master
6. Start slave, verify it catches up
7. Test recovery: kill master, restart, verify slave reconnects

Document the test procedure in TESTING.md.

1783140379.7147233.instruction:
Update README.md with replication setup instructions:

1. Master-Slave Setup:
   - Start master: python server.py --server-id 1
   - Create replication user on master
   - Start slave: python server.py --server-id 2 --role slave --master-host localhost:9999
   - Run START SLAVE on slave

2. Master-Master Setup:
   - Server A: python server.py --server-id 1 --peer-host localhost:9998
   - Server B: python server.py --server-id 2 --peer-host localhost:9999

3. Monitoring commands
4. Troubleshooting section

Include diagram showing replication flow.
